en.extract:
The plumbeous warbler (Setophaga plumbea) is a species of passerine bird belonging to the family Parulidae, the New World warblers. This species is found only in Dominica and Guadeloupe in the Lesser Antilles. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forest and subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest.


Taxonomy
The plumbeous warbler was first formally described as Dendroica plumbea in 1877 by George Newbold Lawrence with its type locality given as Dominica. This species is now classified within the genus Setophaga which belongs to the family Parulidae, the wood warblers or New World warblers. The plumbeous warbler forms a superspecies with the Elfin woods warbler (S. angelae) of Puerto Rico and the arrowhead warbler (S. pharetra) of Jamiaca. 


Etymology
The plumbeous warbler is classified in the genus Setophaga; this name means "moth eater" in Greek. The specific name, plumbea, is Latin and means "lead coloured", a reference to the dull grey, lead-like colour of this bird.


Description
The plumbeous warbler is a small bird with a length of 12 cm (4.7 in). The adults have uniformly grey upperparts with a white supercilium and two white wingbars. The underparts are paler grey with a white belly and centre of the breast. The immatures have greenish-grey upperparts and buffish underparts with less distinct supercilim. The song is made up of between 3 and 12 slow, whistled notes, with the first three being at the same pitch. The call is a dry rattling sound.


Distribution and habitat
The plumbeous warbler is endemic to the Lesser Antilles where it is restricted to Dominica and Guadeloupe, including Marie-Galante and Îles des Saintes. This species is found in humid montane forests as well as in drier scrub and mangroves.

The '''plumbeous warbler''' ('''''Setophaga plumbea''''') is a species of [[passerine]] bird belonging to the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Parulidae]], the New World warblers. This species is found only in Dominica and [[Guadeloupe]] in the [[Lesser Antilles]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical dry forest]] and [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]].

==Taxonomy==
The plumbeous warbler was first formally [[Species description|described]] as ''Dendroica plumbea'' in 1877 by [[George Newbold Lawrence]] with its [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] given as Dominica.<ref name = Lawrence>{{cite journal | vauthors=((Lawrence, George N.)) | journal=Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences | title=Descriptions of new species of birds from the island of Dominica (Art. IV) | volume=1 | pages=46–49 | publisher=New York Academy of Sciences | date= 1879 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/312496 | doi=10.1111/j.1749-6632.1879.tb00274.x}}</ref> This species is now classified within the genus ''[[Setophaga]]'' which belongs to the family Parulidae, the wood warblers or New World warblers.<ref name = Avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=16 October 2025 }}</ref> The plumbeous warbler forms a [[superspecies]] with the [[Elfin woods warbler]] (''S. angelae'') of [[Puerto Rico]] and the [[arrowhead warbler]] (''S. pharetra'') of Jamiaca.<ref name = Curson>{{cite book |author=Jon Curson |author2=David Quinn |name-list-style=and |author3=David Beadle |year=1994 |title=New World Warblers |series=Helm Identification Guides |publisher=Christopher Helm (Publishers) Ltd |pages=154–155 |isbn=0-7136-3932-6}}</ref> 

==Etymology==
The plumbeous warbler is classified in the genus ''Setophaga''; this name means "moth eater" in Greek. The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]], ''plumbea'', is Latin and means "lead coloured", a reference to the dull grey, lead-like colour of this bird.<ref name =job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A. | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location = London, United Kingdom | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | page =[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n355 355][https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n309 309] }}</ref>

==Description==
The plumbeous warbler is a small bird with a length of {{cvt|12|cm}}. The adults have uniformly grey upperparts with a white [[supercilium]] and two white wingbars. The underparts are paler grey with a white belly and centre of the breast. The immatures have greenish-grey upperparts and buffish underparts with less distinct supercilim.<ref name = BWI>{{cite book |author=Herbert Raffaele |author2=James Wiley |author3=Orlando Garrido |author4=Allan Keith |author5=Janis Raffaele |year=2003 |title=Birds of the West Indies |pages=164-165, 170-171 |series = Helm Field Guides |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-7136-5419-6}}</ref> The song is made up of between 3 and 12 slow, whistled notes, with the first three being at the same pitch. The call is a dry rattling sound.<ref name = eBird>{{cite web |url=https://ebird.org/species/pluwar1?siteLanguage=en_GB |title=Plumbeous Warbler ''Setophaga plumbea'' |access-date=13 July 2025 |publisher=[[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]] |work=[[eBird]]}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat== 
The plumbeous warbler is endemic to the Lesser Antilles where it is restricted to Dominica and Guadeloupe, including [[Marie-Galante]] and [[Îles des Saintes]].<ref name = Vaurie>{{cite journal |author=Vaurie, Charles |author-link=Charles Vaurie |year=1961 |title=List of and Notes on the Birds of the Iles Des Saintes, French West Indies |journal=The Auk |volume=78 |issue=1 |pages=57–62 |doi=10.2307/4082234 |jstor=4082234 |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/auk/vol78/iss1/4}}</ref> This species is found in humid [[montane forest]]s as well as in drier scrub and mangroves.<ref name = BWI/>
